title: RPN vs. Selective Search
concept: RPN is a deep learning model that runs on the GPU and learns from data, whereas Selective Search is a classical CPU-bound algorithm with fixed mathematical rules.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main benefit of end-to-end training?

options.text: It optimizes all components of the network simultaneously
It forces the model to run on the CPU
It removes the need for anchor boxes

exercises.explain: End-to-end training allows joint optimization of all parameters in a single pass.

cards.title: Neural vs. Classical
cards.body: If you want to sort mail, you can write a rigid list of rules (like: 'if the stamp is red, put it on the left'). This is Selective Search. Or, you can train a smart robotic sorting arm that learns from experience. This is the Region Proposal Network.

cards.title: RPN vs. Selective Search
cards.body: The Region Proposal Network (RPN) is a data-driven neural network that runs on the GPU and learns to propose regions from training targets. Selective Search is a classical algorithm that runs on the CPU, relying on fixed, hand-crafted pixel similarity heuristics.

cards.title: Autonomous Car vs. Wind-up Toy
cards.body: Selective Search is like a wind-up toy car — it only moves in a fixed straight path, unable to adapt if the floor changes. RPN is like a modern self-driving car — it uses sensors (features) and experience (data) to steer and navigate dynamically.

cards.title: Speed Comparison
cards.body: Selective Search takes about 2,000 milliseconds on a CPU. RPN takes about 10 milliseconds on a GPU, representing a 200x speedup while generating higher-quality proposal coordinates.

cards.title: RPN is not just faster
cards.body: You might think RPN is only popular because it is fast. In reality, because it is trained on the actual target objects in the dataset, RPN proposes far more accurate bounding boxes than Selective Search, which gets distracted by background textures.

cards.title: Algorithm Comparison
cards.body: Comparing classical Selective Search vs the neural RPN:

left.text:
CPU-based, fixed pixel heuristics.
No learning, 2.0s per image.

exercises.prompt: Why does the RPN propose more accurate bounding boxes than Selective Search?
exercises.explain: Because it is part of the neural network, the RPN is trained on the ground truth boxes, learning to locate real objects rather than just color segments.

options.text: It is trained on the actual target dataset objects, allowing it to learn what an object looks like
It converts all images to black and white first
It runs on the CPU to prevent noise

exercises.prompt: True or False: Selective Search is a learning algorithm that adjusts its pixel weights during training.
exercises.explain: False. Selective Search is a classical algorithm with fixed mathematical heuristics; it does not contain weights and does not learn from data.
The RPN runs on the GPU, allowing massive parallel matrix calculations.
exercises.sentence: While Selective Search is a CPU algorithm, RPN runs on the ___.

exercises.prompt: Which feature is unique to the RPN compared to Selective Search?
exercises.explain: The RPN is neural and data-driven, optimizing its weights during backpropagation.

options.text: The ability to learn and adapt its proposal rules based on training labels
Grouping adjacent pixels based on color similarity
Running on the CPU to generate 2,000 crops

exercises.prompt: You are presenting a paper on modern object detection. A reviewer asks why deep learning detectors abandoned classical algorithms like Selective Search. What is your final verdict?

options.text: Classical algorithms cannot learn from data and run slowly on the CPU, while neural proposal networks (like RPN) leverage GPUs and learn to locate objects accurately.
Selective Search is only compatible with old JPEG images.
RPN does not use anchor boxes, making it simpler.
CPUs were phased out of computers.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The speed of GPU execution paired with the accuracy of data-driven learning is why neural RPNs completely replaced classical algorithms. Next: let's learn how we evaluate our object detection models using standard metrics!
